ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Air Mata Kucing. Mapped to the nearest parent class 'beverage food product' (FOODON_03301710). A species-specific entry for this traditional Malaysian/Singaporean herbal longan drink does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific entry for Air Mata Kucing. A0FJP (plant-based beverages / herbal infusions) is the closest defensible category code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this traditional beverage preparation. SNOMED CT coverage for Southeast Asian traditional drinks is absent as of 2024.

main_ingredients: dried monk fruit (luo han guo, Siraitia grosvenorii)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Air Mata Kucing is one cup or glass of approximately 250 ml, typically served chilled over ice with the softened dried longans included.

nutrient_source.title: Longan, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 9174); Monk Fruit / Luo Han Guo compositional data — FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; rock sugar compositional data — USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the prepared Air Mata Kucing beverage. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw longan (FDC ID 9174) as the primary ingredient, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA compositional data for dried longan and monk fruit, and adjusted for dilution in the final beverage (approximately 10–15% solids by weight after boiling). The input record's per_100g values (energy 55 kcal, protein 0.2 g, fat 0.1 g, carbohydrate 13.5 g) are consistent with this diluted-fruit-infusion profile and are retained. Saturated fat is estimated as trace (0.0 g) given the negligible total fat content of the fruit-based beverage.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fruit-based beverages and longan — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fruit juices and longan-containing drinks
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Air Mata Kucing exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) longan juice / fruit-based beverages with moderate sugar content typically falling in the GI 45–55 range in the Sydney database; (2) the moderating effect of dietary fibre (0.8 g/100 g) from dried fruit solids; (3) the use of rock sugar and monk fruit sweetener, which have a lower glycemic impact than equivalent refined sucrose. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 33.75) / 100 = 16.9 (based on 250 g serving with 13.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (5 mg/100 g) with moderate potassium (110 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Air Mata Kucing is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its very low sodium content (5 mg per 100 ml) and moderate potassium contribution from dried longans, which supports a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio; however, portion size should be monitored to avoid excessive sugar intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content (13.5 g/100 g) predominantly as sugars (12.2 g/100 g) with a glycemic load of approximately 16.9 per 250 ml serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Air Mata Kucing in limited quantities; a standard 250 ml serving delivers approximately 33.75 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~16.9, which can meaningfully raise postprandial blood glucose, and the drink should be counted within the daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat content (0.1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and antioxidant compounds from monk fruit and longan
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Air Mata Kucing poses minimal cardiovascular risk due to its negligible fat and zero cholesterol content; the antioxidant mogrosides from monk fruit (luo han guo) may offer mild cardioprotective benefit, though the added sugar content warrants moderation in individuals managing metabolic cardiovascular risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidant compounds (mogrosides, polyphenols) from monk fruit and dried longan
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Air Mata Kucing is generally well-tolerated by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the antioxidant compounds in monk fruit and longan may provide mild anti-inflammatory benefit, and the drink is low in pro-inflammatory saturated fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Air Mata Kucing (literally 'Cat's Eye Water' in Malay) is a traditional cooling herbal drink widely consumed in Malaysia and Singapore. The name derives from the appearance of dried longans (Dimocarpus longan), whose translucent, dark-brown flesh resembles a cat's eye when rehydrated. The drink is prepared by slowly boiling dried longans together with dried monk fruit (luo han guo, Siraitia grosvenorii), and optionally candied winter melon and rock sugar, in water for 30–60 minutes until a dark, richly flavoured infusion is produced. It is almost always served chilled over ice, with the softened longan flesh included in the cup. The flavour profile is naturally sweet, slightly floral, and mildly caramel-like, with a clean finish from the monk fruit. Nutritionally, per 100 ml the drink provides approximately 55 kcal, 13.5 g carbohydrate (of which 12.2 g is sugar), 0.2 g protein, and 0.1 g fat, making it a low-fat, low-protein, carbohydrate-dominant beverage. It contributes modest amounts of vitamin C (12.5 mg/100 ml), potassium (110 mg/100 ml), and iron (0.4 mg/100 ml) from the dried fruit ingredients. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low GI), though the glycemic load per standard 250 ml serving is approximately 16.9, which is clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The drink is vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is deeply embedded in Chinese-Malaysian and Singaporean food culture as a 'cooling' tonic in the traditional Chinese medicine sense, consumed to counteract heat and fatigue, particularly during hot weather or after consuming 'heaty' foods. It is commonly sold at hawker centres, traditional Chinese medicine shops, and roadside stalls throughout Malaysia and Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the prepared Air Mata Kucing beverage as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw longan (FDC ID 9174) and FAO/INFOODS SEA compositional data for dried longan and monk fruit, adjusted for dilution in the final beverage. The input record's values are consistent with this profile and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable fruit-based beverages. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this traditional boiled fruit-infusion beverage is not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA compositional tables as of 2024. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content of the final prepared beverage as served is not reported in the approved sources consulted; the drink is predominantly water by volume but an exact figure is not available from an approved source. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero / naturally zero): dried longan and monk fruit do not contain meaningful retinol or beta-carotene (vitamin A), cobalamin (B12), or cholecalciferol (vitamin D); these are confirmed negligible in USDA FoodData Central longan entries. 4. saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0: with total fat at 0.1 g/100 g, saturated fat is naturally zero / trace for this plant-based fruit infusion. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — this is a plant-based beverage with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats are present. 7. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for Air Mata Kucing exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. The input source (MyHealth Ministry of Health Malaysia) is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary nutrient evidence; USDA FoodData Central and FAO/INFOODS SEA are used instead. 9. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 A0FJP are approximate matches only; no exact ontology code exists for this dish.
